Q1: Honestly, my study routine was kind of all over the place. I tried to set aside time after work and on weekends, but life kept getting in the way. I'd tell myself I'd study for two hours, but then I'd get distracted or feel too tired or just... sad, I guess. Sometimes I'd watch the videos late at night when I couldn't sleep because my mind was racing with anxiety. It wasn't organized at all, more like grabbing moments when I could.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and how I was feeling emotionally. Some weeks I'd be on top of things, logging in almost every day. Other weeks, especially when my anxiety was really bad, I'd avoid everything until the last minute. Deadlines were really the only thing that forced any consistency. Without them, I'd just drift and lose track of time.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline that got me to log in. I'd suddenly remember something was due and rush to the LMS to figure out what I needed to do. Sometimes the peer support group would message me and remind me about things, which helped. Occasionally I'd log in just to watch videos because it felt like I was doing something productive even when I couldn't bring myself to actually complete assignments.
Q4: For a little while, checking the LMS in the morning became kind of automatic. I'd wake up, make coffee, and just scroll through what was posted. But that routine fell apart when things got harder emotionally. Once I started feeling really low, even that small habit felt like too much effort. The peer support connections helped bring me back sometimes, but it was never really stable.
Q5: The videos were the most useful for me because I learn better by watching and doing rather than reading. I could pause them, rewind, and actually see concepts demonstrated. The peer interactions were also helpful when I actually participated because hearing other people's perspectives made things click. Discussion posts felt less useful to me personally since writing my thoughts out is hard when I'm anxious.
Q6: I barely touched the readings and supplementary materials. Without internet at home, I couldn't always access them easily, and downloading everything at the library was a hassle. I also ignored a lot of the optional practice quizzes because they felt overwhelming when I was already behind on the required stuff. It's not that I didn't think they'd help—I just couldn't handle adding more to my plate.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it directly connected to an assignment. Like, if watching a video helped me understand what I needed to do for homework, that felt valuable. But if something seemed abstract or disconnected from what was being graded, I'd skip it. I needed to feel like my limited energy was going somewhere that mattered.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. When you're already struggling to complete the required stuff and dealing with anxiety, optional feels like 'for people who have their lives together.' Required activities had this weight to them—I had to do them or fail. Optional things just faded into the background no matter how helpful they might have been.
Q9: Yeah, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download videos and PDFs at the library since I don't have internet at home, then study from my laptop or printed notes. I'd also work through problems by hand in a notebook while watching downloaded lectures. A lot of my actual learning happened in my apartment, completely disconnected from the LMS.
Q10: There were many times I'd be going over my handwritten notes or rewatching a downloaded video multiple times to understand something, but none of that showed up anywhere. I'd spend hours working through practice problems on paper. Sometimes I'd study with a peer from the support group in person, and we'd work through concepts together. The LMS only saw me when I uploaded something or clicked around online.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download a lecture once, then watch it three or four times offline over the next week. Same with readings—I'd save them as PDFs and mark them up on my tablet or print them out. The single access the LMS recorded didn't reflect how many times I actually engaged with that material. It was just more practical given my internet situation.
Q12: There were weeks when my anxiety was so bad I could barely function. I'd lie in bed feeling paralyzed, knowing I should be studying but unable to make myself move. Financial stress made it worse—worrying about bills while trying to focus on coursework felt impossible. During those times, even logging into the LMS felt like climbing a mountain. Everything just felt heavy and pointless.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from work or overwhelmed by everything I had to do, the LMS became this thing I avoided. Confusion about assignment instructions made me anxious, so I'd put things off instead of asking for help. Competing responsibilities—work, family stuff, just surviving—meant the course often got pushed aside. My LMS usage dropped whenever life got chaotic.
Q14: So many times. I'd log in and watch videos but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. I'd be thinking about money problems or just feeling this heavy sadness, and the content wouldn't sink in at all. I'd finish a video and realize I couldn't remember anything from it. Being physically present on the platform didn't mean I was actually learning anything.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The anxiety made starting things feel impossible—like this wall I couldn't climb over. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and tomorrow would come and I'd feel the same way. It wasn't laziness; it was more like being frozen. The bigger the assignment, the more I'd avoid it.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me engage. I'd ignore the LMS for days, then suddenly realize something was due in 24 hours and panic-study. It wasn't healthy, but the pressure was the only thing strong enough to break through my avoidance. My LMS activity probably looked like nothing, nothing, nothing, then a huge spike right before due dates.
Q17: Feedback was complicated for me. Good feedback motivated me briefly, but negative feedback—even constructive stuff—would send my anxiety spiraling. I'd avoid checking grades for days because I was scared of what I'd see. When I did get encouraging comments, it helped me feel like maybe I could do this. But the fear of criticism often kept me from engaging with feedback at all.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which was both good and bad for me. Good because I could work around my schedule and lack of internet. Bad because without strict structure, I'd drift and lose momentum. I needed more external accountability than the course provided. The peer support helped fill that gap somewhat, but I still struggled with the freedom.
Q19: Having videos instead of just readings made engagement easier since I learn better that way. But the assignment instructions were sometimes confusing, which triggered my anxiety and made me avoid starting them. The discussion requirements felt hard because posting publicly when you're already anxious is terrifying. Shorter, more frequent check-ins might have helped me stay on track better.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well, actually. I was on the LMS a lot—watching videos, reading things, clicking around—but my assignment completion was low. For me, it was anxiety and avoidance. I was trying to learn, but the actual submission part felt like this huge barrier. Consuming content felt safer than producing work that would be judged.
Q21: I can see how that happens. If someone studies mostly offline like I do, their LMS activity would look minimal even if they're putting in real work. Or maybe they're just efficient—they know exactly what they need, grab it quickly, and study elsewhere. The LMS doesn't capture studying from downloaded materials or working with peers outside the platform.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they miss everything else. They miss the emotional state you were in, whether you actually absorbed anything, all the offline studying, the conversations with peers outside the system. They show surface-level activity but not depth of engagement. They definitely can't tell if someone is struggling mentally while technically being 'active.'
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine effort to understand it. It's not just clicking through videos or submitting assignments—it's wrestling with ideas and wanting to improve. For me, real engagement happened in quiet moments with my notes, not necessarily when I was visible on the LMS. It's internal as much as it is external.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I was fighting just to keep going. It wouldn't show the anxiety attacks before assignments, the sadness that made everything feel pointless, or the offline hours I spent trying to understand material. It wouldn't show that the peer support connections were sometimes the only thing keeping me from giving up completely. The data shows what I did, not what it cost me to do it.
